body_txt: Because nothing says decadent and evil like death and carnage as a spectator sport. People — criminals or slaves — are forced to fight each other or savage monsters. Sword and Sorcery (especially Sword and Sandal ) and Lost World stories are particularly fond of the monsters; a good way to show them off. As a Blood Sport , this may last for a time, with many bouts, allowing the gladiators time to train; either the character can always win, or they do not always insist on death at defeat. The first is more common ; the crowds will insist Finish Him! Conversely, it may be a way of combining executions with amusement — a Death Trap that gets watched — which generally involves animals, as they don't want to deal with the winner. Monsters used in an arena will fight long after a Real Life animal would retreat. This is often a result of being starved a bit leading up to the match, as was the practice in the Real Life games. Alternatively, some stories may have the animals or monsters being unnaturally aggressive due to being drugged, mind-controlled, tortured or modified in some other way to be made more violent, fearless or angry than they normally would be, thus explaining why they’re so determined to keep trying to kill the gladiator, even if the gladiator is obviously stronger than they are. In Science Fiction and Science Fantasy , Bioweapon Beasts deliberately engineered for hyperagression and showy combat are also likely to be used. Because gladiator combat is ultimately a form of spectacle and entertainment, visual appeal and a "wow factor" tend to win out over practicality in-universe. A common result of this is that gladiator armor tends to be created with an eye for display and to often be highly impractical to wear. It may be heavy, intimidating, and ornate to the point of being highly cumbersome or, more often, is deliberately exposing in order to display the gladiator's prowess or just make a showier display of their eventual messy death — Roman gladiators, for instance, had helmets and arm armor but went bare-chested and -bellied. This trope usually involves the aesthetics of Roman gladiators and arenas — down to the name which comes from the Roman gladius stabbing sword. The games are a common element in historical stories set in Rome itself and in Fantasy Counterpart Cultures of it. Other examples can involve various other aesthetics, from vicious underworld pit fights to elaborate, publicized, high-tech displays. Futuristic and retro-futuristic gladiator games are also popular in Space Opera settings, with fighters coloring the sands of alien arenas with exotic shades of blood as they clash with each other and with alien monsters. In all of these cases, this trope is distinguished from other forms of recreational combat — such as The Tourney — by one or both sides having no choice but to fight. Successful gladiators may become figures of note — professional Roman gladiators could notably amass signficant wealth and public renown, assuming that they lived to enjoy it, and many fictional arena fighters follow suit. Sometimes, after a particularly grand victory, the gladiator is freed , but generally ends with either a Gladiator Revolt , or the gladiators loosing the monsters they fight upon the crowd, to great consternation. On the other hand, this situation was involved in the Trope Namer for Androcles' Lion . Frequently features in the nastier sort of Bread and Circuses , but it may just be an upper-class entertainment because Aristocrats Are Evil . Contrast The Tourney , which doesn't carry this stigma because the nobles risk themselves rather than compelling others to do so, and as a result also usually doesn't involve people dying. Very popular in Ancient Rome settings and in Sword and Sandal settings based on it, although Hollywood History comes into play for some of the elements. For more info, see the Analysis page . When gladiatorial combat appears in a modern setting, it is often a Deadly Game or a Blood Sport . Will often take place in a ThunderDome of some sort and involve a Spectator Casualty or two. Compare to Trial by Combat , which shares many similarities. See also Combat Chariot and Chariot Race which are often associated with Gladiator spectacles. Contrast with the Lighter and Softer and longer-lived Greek counterpart, The Olympics . When good guys are forced to participate in death matches, they typically refuse to kill their opponent, finding a way to get out of it.
